raw_response: {"summary":"Live search suggests a remaining distinct first-pass feature direction: fit simple physical SED templates to ugriz photometry and use target-free fit parameters/residuals as features.","hypotheses":[{"title":"Analytic SED Template Residual Features","summary":"Convert ugriz magnitudes into normalized flux SEDs, fit simple blackbody and power-law continua, and use the fit parameters and residuals as class-separating features.","feature_family":"physical_sed_template_residual_features","feature_strategy":"Keep raw numeric columns, one-hot spectral_type and galaxy_population, and basic adjacent colors only as anchors, then add a target-free SED-template representation. Convert u,g,r,i,z magnitudes to relative f_nu fluxes using fixed SDSS effective wavelengths, normalize each object's 5-band flux vector by total flux or r-band flux, and fit two small analytic template grids: blackbody curves over plausible stellar temperatures and power-law continua f_nu proportional to nu^alpha over a compact alpha grid. Fit only an amplitude per template by least squares, optionally repeat using rest-frame wavelengths lambda_obs/(1+redshift clipped safely). Add best blackbody temperature index/value, best power-law alpha, per-family RMSE/MAE/max residual, blackbody-vs-power-law error ratio, signed residuals per band, residual curvature across adjacent bands, and continuum-excess proxies that may capture emission-line or dropout behavior. Do not use class labels, target encodings, auxiliary labels, or supervised intermediate models.","baseline_model_panel":"Use a simple reusable balanced multiclass panel: a regularized multinomial linear model on standardized dense features, a shallow class-weighted tree ensemble, and one class-balanced gradient-boosted tree model suitable for mixed numeric/categorical features such as CatBoost with auto_class_weights='Balanced' or LightGBM with class_weight='balanced'.","model_panel_rationale":"The linear model tests whether template-fit coordinates are directly separable, the shallow tree ensemble tests threshold/interactions in residual patterns, and the balanced boosted tree checks whether the engineered residuals add nonlinear signal without making the hypothesis about tuning or ensembling.","validation_strategy":"Use 5-fold StratifiedKFold on the training set and report balanced accuracy per fold and per model family; all scalers and any template-feature normalizers that learn distributional constants should be fit within each fold, while fixed physical wavelengths/template grids are global constants.","materialization_hint":"Implement a feature builder that vectorizes magnitude-to-flux conversion, handles finite clipping, computes template fits by matrix operations against precomputed blackbody and power-law grids, appends the resulting fit summaries/residuals to the base feature frame, and then feeds the same train/test matrices to the baseline panel. Keep template grids small enough for fast iteration and log feature ablations for observed-frame versus redshift-rest-frame versions.","expected_signal":"If useful, CV should improve most for STAR-vs-QSO and QSO-vs-GALAXY confusion cases, with tree models assigning importance to best-fit temperature/alpha, continuum error ratios, and band residuals; a weak signal would show near-zero gains over plain colors and high correlation with existing flux features.","novelty_confidence":"medium","risk":"This overlaps partially with prior physical flux and stellar-locus hypotheses because it reuses ugriz flux shape, but the main mechanism is different: explicit analytic continuum template fitting and residual diagnostics rather than flux moments, empirical loci, or published color cuts. Broad five-band photometry may be too coarse for reliable physical fits, and synthetic competition generation may not preserve true blackbody or quasar power-law structure.","sources":["https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sloan_Digital_Sky_Survey","https://arxiv.org/abs/astro-ph/0012449","https://arxiv.org/abs/astro-ph/0404358","https://arxiv.org/abs/1508.04121"]}]}

parsed_response.summary: Live search suggests a remaining distinct first-pass feature direction: fit simple physical SED templates to ugriz photometry and use target-free fit parameters/residuals as features.

hypotheses.title: Analytic SED Template Residual Features
hypotheses.summary: Convert ugriz magnitudes into normalized flux SEDs, fit simple blackbody and power-law continua, and use the fit parameters and residuals as class-separating features.
hypotheses.feature_strategy: Keep raw numeric columns, one-hot spectral_type and galaxy_population, and basic adjacent colors only as anchors, then add a target-free SED-template representation. Convert u,g,r,i,z magnitudes to relative f_nu fluxes using fixed SDSS effective wavelengths, normalize each object's 5-band flux vector by total flux or r-band flux, and fit two small analytic template grids: blackbody curves over plausible stellar temperatures and power-law continua f_nu proportional to nu^alpha over a compact alpha grid. Fit only an amplitude per template by least squares, optionally repeat using rest-frame wavelengths lambda_obs/(1+redshift clipped safely). Add best blackbody temperature index/value, best power-law alpha, per-family RMSE/MAE/max residual, blackbody-vs-power-law error ratio, signed residuals per band, residual curvature across adjacent bands, and continuum-excess proxies that may capture emission-line or dropout behavior. Do not use class labels, target encodings, auxiliary labels, or supervised intermediate models.
hypotheses.baseline_model_panel: Use a simple reusable balanced multiclass panel: a regularized multinomial linear model on standardized dense features, a shallow class-weighted tree ensemble, and one class-balanced gradient-boosted tree model suitable for mixed numeric/categorical features such as CatBoost with auto_class_weights='Balanced' or LightGBM with class_weight='balanced'.
hypotheses.model_panel_rationale: The linear model tests whether template-fit coordinates are directly separable, the shallow tree ensemble tests threshold/interactions in residual patterns, and the balanced boosted tree checks whether the engineered residuals add nonlinear signal without making the hypothesis about tuning or ensembling.
hypotheses.validation_strategy: Use 5-fold StratifiedKFold on the training set and report balanced accuracy per fold and per model family; all scalers and any template-feature normalizers that learn distributional constants should be fit within each fold, while fixed physical wavelengths/template grids are global constants.
hypotheses.materialization_hint: Implement a feature builder that vectorizes magnitude-to-flux conversion, handles finite clipping, computes template fits by matrix operations against precomputed blackbody and power-law grids, appends the resulting fit summaries/residuals to the base feature frame, and then feeds the same train/test matrices to the baseline panel. Keep template grids small enough for fast iteration and log feature ablations for observed-frame versus redshift-rest-frame versions.
hypotheses.expected_signal: If useful, CV should improve most for STAR-vs-QSO and QSO-vs-GALAXY confusion cases, with tree models assigning importance to best-fit temperature/alpha, continuum error ratios, and band residuals; a weak signal would show near-zero gains over plain colors and high correlation with existing flux features.
hypotheses.risk: This overlaps partially with prior physical flux and stellar-locus hypotheses because it reuses ugriz flux shape, but the main mechanism is different: explicit analytic continuum template fitting and residual diagnostics rather than flux moments, empirical loci, or published color cuts. Broad five-band photometry may be too coarse for reliable physical fits, and synthetic competition generation may not preserve true blackbody or quasar power-law structure.
